tier: CRITICAL
journey_name: File Notice of Loss after named storm
trigger: Owner gets a loss alert that a named storm hit their insured property
narrative: The owner opens the dashboard, sees the loss alert, reviews the storm incident snapshot for their property, drafts a Notice of Loss pre-filled from their policy and the storm data, files it, and it shows up in their case manager's pipeline.
steps: See alert; open incident snapshot; draft NOL; review pre-filled fields; file NOL; see Filed status; (case manager) see it in pipeline
systems_touched: claims-client; claims-server; named-storm engine; NOL drafter; claims-management-client pipeline
success_outcome: NOL status reads 'Filed' and a NOL record exists and is visible in the case manager pipeline
parity_baseline: A public adjuster hand-keying the loss from a phone call — we must capture every field they would and faster
failure_modes: No policy on file; duplicate filing for same loss; storm not matched to property
preconditions: Authenticated owner; property with uploaded policy; a named storm intersecting the property
data_created: NOL record; pipeline entry; audit log row
help_needed: Owner doesn't know what a 'Notice of Loss' is; unsure which storm caused damage

tier: # HOW TO USE THIS FILE
#   1. Keep the header row (line 1). Delete the J-NOL-FILE example row above once you understand it.
#   2. Add ONE row per journey. A journey = a whole task a real person finishes
#   3. See QA/journeys-how-to.md "Step 1 — fill in the journey map" for what each column means.
# COLUMN CHEAT-SHEET (full guide in the how-to):
#   tier            CRITICAL (must work to ship) | IMPORTANT (tracked defect) | DIFFERENTIATOR (why switch to us)
#   journey_id      J-<DOMAIN>-<VERB>
#   journey_name    short human title
#   persona         who is doing this (Property owner | Case manager | Property manager | ...)
#   trigger         the real-world event that starts it ("owner gets a storm alert")
#   narrative       2-3 sentences
journey_id: trigger -> outcome. If it doesn't cross 2+ systems & end in a saved

tier: #                   state change
#   steps           the meaningful beats in order
#   systems_touched the apps/engines involved
#   success_outcome THE ORACLE. One checkable fact: a record/status/number/redirect. NOT a feeling.
#                   Good: "status reads 'Filed' and a NOL row exists in the pipeline"
#                   Bad:  "the owner feels confident"
#   parity_baseline how a person does this TODAY (adjuster / spreadsheet / carrier portal). This sorts the tier.
#   failure_modes   how it goes wrong in real life
#   preconditions   what must already be true before the trigger
#   data_created    what records exist afterward
#   help_needed     where a real user gets confused (informs UI copy / tooltips)
